one_line_takeaway: Machine-unlearning methods for LLMs (GA, NPO, RMU) that pass static single-turn safety evaluation on hazardous WMDP knowledge frequently leak the 'forgotten' knowledge back out under ordinary, non-adversarial multi-turn self-correction or dialogue context, and the methods that resist this leakage (like RMU) do so mainly via behavioral rigidity and heavy utility loss rather than genuine knowledge removal.
key_finding: On WMDP-Bio (Table 1), GA and NPO variants show large self-correction recovery: e.g. Llama-NPO+GD Round-1 accuracy is 25.37 but jumps to 70.86 under S1 (ΔAns 99.89, i.e. the model changes its answer on almost all previously-wrong examples when told it was wrong); under S2 (answer excluded), conditional accuracy still reaches 60-67% for most GA/NPO variants, well above the 33% random-guess floor for the reduced candidate set; under S3 (mere 'reconsider' prompt with no correctness signal), accuracy also rises substantially (e.g. Llama base R1 66.93 vs S3 R2 65.51, and unlearned variants show ΔAns in the 17-33 range). RMU is comparatively stable (Llama-RMU: R1 26.87, R2_S1 31.89, R2_S3 26.79, S1 ΔAns only 17.62) but the paper interprets this as behavioral rigidity/reduced instruction-following rather than genuine erasure. Table 2 (dialogue-conditioned, WMDP-Bio) shows accuracy recovery for LLaMA regardless of whether prior dialogue content is semantically/structurally related to the forget set (e.g. Llama-NPO+KL: Base 25.77 -> 32.99 under WMDP-Bio MCQ context, 30.64 under unrelated MMLU QA context), while for Zephyr structural (MCQ-format) alignment is the dominant driver of recovery (e.g. Zephyr-NPO+KL: Base 24.98 -> 35.04 WMDP-Bio MCQ vs 24.67 MMLU MCQ). RMU shows comparatively small or even negative accuracy shifts under dialogue conditioning (Llama-RMU Base 27.18 vs WMDP-Bio MCQ 24.67). Table 3 shows RMU's asymmetric utility cost: Llama-RMU MMLU utility collapses from Base 55.22 to 22.95 when dialogue context is drawn from the WMDP-Bio forget domain (both MCQ and QA), while GA/NPO utility generally improves modestly under dialogue conditioning. The learning-rate ablations (Figs. 2-5) show that increasing unlearning strength lowers R2 accuracy and ΔAns together, which the authors interpret as reduced interaction sensitivity (rigidity) rather than deeper knowledge removal -- i.e., stronger unlearning trades adaptability/utility for apparent (not genuine) robustness.
core_contribution: The paper introduces and empirically studies a novel problem: whether LLM machine-unlearning methods that appear to successfully suppress hazardous/forgotten knowledge under standard static single-turn evaluation remain robust when probed through realistic multi-turn interaction. It defines two interaction patterns not previously used to stress-test unlearning: (1) self-correction, a controlled two-round protocol in which a model's Round-1 answer is challenged or the model is asked to reconsider, and (2) dialogue-conditioned querying, where a final evaluation question is appended after a multi-turn dialogue history that varies in semantic relatedness and structural (format) similarity to the unlearning target. The authors run these protocols against three representative unlearning methods (Gradient Ascent/GA, Negative Preference Optimization/NPO, and Representation Misdirection for Unlearning/RMU) on two open-weight models, and additionally run ablations varying unlearning strength (learning rate) to characterize a robustness/adaptability trade-off.
threat_model: Non-adversarial, realistic end-user interaction rather than adversarial jailbreaking: an ordinary user who (a) tells the unlearned model its answer was wrong and asks it to try again or reconsider (error-aware self-correction, with and without excluding the prior answer from the candidate set; and introspective self-correction, e.g. 'are you sure?'), or (b) simply has an extended prior conversation (which may be on-topic, off-topic, MCQ-formatted, or QA-formatted) before asking the target question. The paper explicitly frames this as distinct from prior adversarial jailbreak-style multi-turn attacks (citing Li et al. 2024a) and from in-context relearning attacks (Lynch et al. 2024) -- those are related work, not this paper's own contribution. The 'harmful output' framing centers on WMDP-Bio and WMDP-Cyber content (biosecurity- and cybersecurity-relevant hazardous knowledge) that unlearning is meant to suppress.
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors: the study covers only two mid-sized instruction-tuned models (LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct and Zephyr-7B-Beta); models of different scale or training paradigm may behave differently. Evaluation is restricted to multiple-choice question (MCQ) settings. Findings may depend on the specific prompting templates used. (Additional implicit scope limits visible in the paper: only three unlearning methods -- GA, NPO, RMU -- are tested; the rule-based dialogue simulator, not real human users, generates the multi-turn histories; and self-correction protocol is a fixed controlled two-round design rather than open-ended multi-turn dialogue.)

datasets_used: WMDP (Weapons of Mass Destruction Proxy) benchmark -- WMDP-Bio and WMDP-Cyber subsets, used as the unlearning target
Wikidata, used as the retain set for GD/KL regularization
MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding), used as the general-utility evaluation benchmark
MMLU-Sociology subset, used to construct structurally-aligned but semantically-unrelated dialogue histories
quantitative_results: Table 1 (WMDP-Bio self-correction): Llama base R1=66.93 -> R2_S3=65.51; Llama-NPO+GD R1=25.37 -> R2_S1=70.86 with S1 ΔAns=99.89; Llama-RMU R1=26.87 -> R2_S1=31.89 with S1 ΔAns only 17.62 (most rigid/least responsive)
Table 1: S2 conditional accuracy for GA/NPO variants clusters around 60-67% versus a 33% random-guess floor after excluding the Round-1 option

Table 2 (WMDP-Bio dialogue-conditioned): Llama-NPO+KL Base=25.77 -> WMDP-Bio MCQ context=32.99; Zephyr-NPO+KL Base=24.98 -> WMDP-Bio MCQ context=35.04 vs MMLU MCQ context=24.67, showing structural-format alignment drives recovery for Zephyr

Table 3 (MMLU utility under dialogue conditioning): Llama-RMU utility collapses from Base=55.22 to 22.95 under WMDP-Bio MCQ/QA dialogue context, while remaining near baseline (60.85/61.16) under unrelated MMLU-domain context
baselines_compared: Gradient Ascent (GA), with GD and KL regularization variants
Negative Preference Optimization (NPO), with GD and KL regularization variants
Original (non-unlearned) base models (Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Zephyr-7B-Beta) as reference points
Static single-turn ('Base') evaluation as the baseline interaction condition compared against self-correction and dialogue-conditioned evaluation
Few-shot in-context-learning conditioning (static ground-truth examples) as a comparison point against dynamic dialogue interaction (results in Appendix I)
